old_claim.note: Global Climate Control Treaties is a plausible dependency for a forecast technology and should be treated as speculative.

new_claim.note: IoT and ubiquitous connectivity provide the networked sensor, software, internet, and data-exchange substrate for linking distributed remote and in-situ instruments.

source_shape.source_locator: Background and Description: JPL defines a sensorweb as a network of sensors linked by software and the internet, and as networked instruments whose sensor information automatically reconfigures other sensors.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: NASA/JPL frames sensorwebs as networked sensors linked by software and the internet, with sensor information driving automatic reconfiguration of other instruments.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports IoT and ubiquitous connectivity as the direct networking substrate, while global climate treaties are governance consumers or coordinators rather than a technical prerequisite for sensor-web construction.

ontology_before: The graph made planetary-scale sensor webs depend on global climate control treaties, implying governance agreements were required before the sensing network could exist.
ontology_after: The graph routes planetary-scale sensor webs through IoT and ubiquitous connectivity, keeping treaties as downstream governance or application context rather than technical prerequisite infrastructure.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: planetary_scale_sensor_webs now depends on internet_of_things_iot_ubiquitous_connectivity and no longer directly on global_climate_control_treaties.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to treaty verification institutions rather than dense technical monitoring networks.
A source shows global climate-control treaties are a direct prerequisite for building planetary sensor webs rather than a downstream use case.
short_reason: IoT connectivity is the direct sensor-network substrate; climate treaties are downstream governance context.
